evidence_items.value: libavfilter/vf_vmafmotion.c

source_refs.function_context: int ff_vmafmotion_init(VMAFMotionData *s, int w, int h, enum AVPixelFormat fmt)
source_refs.snippet: int ff_vmafmotion_init(...) { ... s->width = w; s->height = h; ... }

local_validation.description: Compare parent of 82ad1b76751b (4c1afa2925) vs 82ad1b76751b at libavfilter/vf_vmafmotion.c ff_vmafmotion_init. Vulnerable version has unconditional s->width=w; fixed version adds guard.
local_validation.check_at_tag: Verify the guard line exists at function entry in the fixed version and is absent in vulnerable version.

block_reasons: Evidence identifies root-cause file but does not by itself prove exploitability in every affected version; version-specific verification is needed to confirm the vulnerable code path is reachable in each tagged release.

evidence_items.value: ff_vmafmotion_init

source_refs.function_context: int ff_vmafmotion_init(VMAFMotionData *s, int w, int h, enum AVPixelFormat fmt)
source_refs.snippet: int ff_vmafmotion_init(...) { ... s->width = w; s->height = h; ... }

local_validation.description: ff_vmafmotion_init is the only function that accepts and stores width/height parameters w and h into s->width/s->height. The fix adds the guard at the top of this function.
local_validation.check_at_tag: Read the function at each tag; check whether guard line exists.

block_reasons: Function-level evidence narrows the fix location but version-specific snapshot is needed to determine fix presence per tag.

evidence_items.value: Missing dimension validation allows convolution with undersized frames (w<3 or h<3), causing out-of-bounds array access in 5-tap filter border clamping logic

source_refs.snippet: int radius = filt_w / 2; int borders_top = radius; int borders_bottom = h - (filt_w - radius); ... for (i = 0; i < borders_top; i++) ... i_tap = h - (i_tap - h + 1) ... where h < 3 leads to negative/out-of-bounds indices

local_validation.description: When filt_w=5, radius=2. borders_bottom = h - (5-2) = h-3. If h=1 or h=2, borders_bottom < 0, the border and middle loops' index arithmetic (FFABS mirror clamp for tap positions near edge) accesses src[i_tap * src_stride + j] with i_tap outside [0, h-1]. Same logic for convolution_x with w.
local_validation.check_at_tag: Validate that the convolution_y/convolution_x function definitions in vf_vmafmotion.c at each tag have unchanged 5-tap kernel logic. The vulnerable path exists wherever the guard in ff_vmafmotion_init is absent.

block_reasons: The vulnerable sequence logic is in the convolution macro, which is stable across versions; the decisive factor is presence/absence of the guard in ff_vmafmotion_init.

evidence_items.value: if (w < 3 || h < 3) return AVERROR(EINVAL);

local_validation.description: The fix adds exactly two lines: 'if (w < 3 || h < 3)' and 'return AVERROR(EINVAL);' inside ff_vmafmotion_init at the fix location. The guard can be verified by grepping for these lines in the function.
local_validation.check_at_tag: grep for 'if (w < 3 || h < 3)' in libavfilter/vf_vmafmotion.c at each tag. Presence = fixed, absence = vulnerable.

block_reasons: The guard presence is a binary check per tag, making it suitable as a fix anchor. However, a hard_certificate requires verifying that no other code path bypasses the guard.

evidence_items.value: Family_semantics=or_backport_bundle: all 6 fix commits are identical patches across different branches (cherry-picks). 82ad1b76751b is the original commit; the others are backports.

local_validation.description: Multiple commits' messages state '(cherry picked from commit 82ad1b...)', confirming backport nature. All 6 patches produce the same 2-line guard addition at the same location.
local_validation.check_at_tag: Each fix commit can be verified independently by checking the guard at the corresponding parent.

block_reasons: Backport structure informs tag propagation but does not directly prove vulnerability or fix presence in any specific release tag.

evidence_items.value: s->lambda = FFMIN(s->lambda * ratio, 65536.f); — one-sided upper clamp, no lower bound, in aacenc.c:652 (pre-fix code)
av_clipf(s->lambda * ratio, FLT_MIN, 65536.f) — two-sided clamp ensures lambda >= FLT_MIN > 0
float distfact = ((sce->ics.num_windows > 1) ? 85.80 : 147.84) / lambda; — division by lambda, dividing a constant by lambda (aaccoder.c:843)
libavcodec/aacenc.c — the file containing the missing lower-bound clamp for s->lambda
Commit message: 'avcodec/aacenc: Avoid 0 lambda' — explicitly confirms zero is the problem
block_reasons: commit message alone is confirmatory but not sufficient as a hard certificate

evidence_items.value: FFMIN provides only one-sided upper clamping; av_clipf provides two-sided range clipping. The replacement of FFMIN with av_clipf + FLT_MIN is the archetypal 'missing_lower_bound' fix pattern.

evidence_items.value: RegisterStaticModules() called register_module() for every static module without any IsRightsAuthorized check, bypassing module policy for bulk initialization

local_validation.observed: RegisterStaticModules body with no IsRightsAuthorized call

evidence_items.value: RegisterStaticModule() used ReadPolicyRights (0x01) as the IsRightsAuthorized rights mask; IsRightsAuthorized only checks bits set in the mask, so WritePolicyRights was never validated
OpenModule() used ReadPolicyRights (0x01) as the rights mask, same under-specification bug as RegisterStaticModule

IsRightsAuthorized(const PolicyRights rights, ...) only checks rights bits that are SET in the 'rights' parameter. Bits not set in the mask are silently skipped — each of Read/Write/Execute checks has an independent 'if ((rights & BIT) != 0)' guard.

local_validation.observed: Each right bit has independent guard; bits not set in 'rights' parameter are never checked
local_validation.implication: rights=ReadPolicyRights (0x01) triggers only the Read check; Write and Execute checks are skipped

evidence_items.value: Corrected rights mask from ReadPolicyRights (0x01) and AllPolicyRights (0xff) to ReadPolicyRights|WritePolicyRights (0x03) in all three functions

evidence_items.value: The out: cleanup if(result && ctx->ssl_ctx) treats CURLE_OK (value 0) as false. When result is 0, SSL_CTX is not freed and function returns CURLE_OK. This invariant makes missing assignments exploitable.

local_validation.method: Same out: label in vulnerable and fixed versions; not changed by patch

block_reasons: The out: label is not a vulnerability itself; it is an invariant the missing assignments interact with. Root cause is the 4 missing result assignments.

Removal of free(buf); — the fix is a single-line deletion. No added guard sequence exists.
buf is a local stack variable (char buf[4]) and does not need free(). The function returns immediately after the error path, so the stack frame is reclaimed automatically.

evidence_items.value: modules/metadata/mod_remoteip.c

local_validation.result: confirmed: patch modifies only modules/metadata/mod_remoteip.c in source code; CHANGES and STATUS are metadata files

evidence_items.value: remoteip_modify_request

local_validation.result: confirmed: patch is inside remoteip_modify_request; function context confirmed via hunk header and parent source read

evidence_items.value: temp_sa = c->client_addr;

local_validation.result: confirmed: this is the only removed line in the source patch; it unconditionally sets temp_sa to c->client_addr, which is the TCP connection address reused on subrequests

evidence_items.value: temp_sa = r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr;

local_validation.result: confirmed: the added line guards against re-processing on subrequests by preferring r->useragent_addr (already set from first pass) over c->client_addr

evidence_items.value: Once r->useragent_addr is set on the main request, it must not be re-derivable from c->client_addr on subrequests. r->useragent_addr is the authoritative user-agent address; c->client_addr is only the TCP connection address and is invariant across subrequests, making it an incorrect trust anchor for re-verification.

source_refs.snippet: When using r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address instead of c->client_addr, the internal redirection uses the first non-trusted IP in this particular case, so it won't change the r->useragent_ip during the internal redirection to ErrorDocument.

local_validation.result: confirmed: the fix enforces the invariant that r->useragent_addr, once set, becomes the trust anchor; the commit message explicitly describes the subrequest re-processing problem

evidence_items.value: Commit message confirms internal subrequest/ErrorDocument scenario: the mod_remoteip handler is executed again with the same c->client_addr as the original request, causing bad useragent_ip to be set.

source_refs.snippet: This fixes issue resulting in setting of bad useragent_ip when internal redirection has been generated as response to the request (typically as result of "ErrorDocument 40x"). In this case, the original request has been handled by mod_remoteip and its useragent_ip has been changed properly, but when internal redirection to ErrorDocument has been generated later, the mod_remoteip's handler has been executed again with *the same* c->client_addr as in the original request. If c->client_addr IP is trusted, this results in bad useragent_ip being set.

local_validation.result: confirmed: the commit message directly describes the subrequest re-execution scenario and c->client_addr reuse as the root cause

block_reasons: commit message is contextual narrative, not source-level evidence; use git_diff evidence for priority scoring

evidence_items.value: __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist) in unix_gc() destroys SKBs carrying io_uring registered files without excluding them, leading to UAF when io_uring accesses freed file structures

local_validation.patch_context: The fix inserts a guard loop immediately before this line to extract io_uring SKBs from hitlist before purge. No removed lines — confirms the purge was always unsafe for io_uring SKBs.
nearby_dangerous_operations: skb->sk = sk; skb->destructor = unix_destruct_scm; refcount_add — in __io_scm_file_account, these link the SKB into Unix socket lifecycle and make it participate in unix_gc reference tracking

evidence_items.value: struct sk_buff scm_io_uring:1 bit flag added to distinguish io_uring-registered-file SKBs from regular SCM SKBs

local_validation.patch_context: Added as a single bit field in sk_buff bitfield region. Also has kerneldoc comment @scm_io_uring: SKB holds io_uring registered files.
used_by: net/unix/garbage.c:unix_gc checks skb->scm_io_uring to exclude from purge

evidence_items.value: skb->scm_io_uring = 1; added in __io_scm_file_account to tag SKBs that carry io_uring registered files

local_validation.patch_context: Inserted after skb->sk = sk and before skb->destructor = unix_destruct_scm; ensures all io_uring SCM SKBs are tagged before entering Unix socket lifecycle.

evidence_items.value: Guard loop before __skb_queue_purge extracts scm_io_uring SKBs from hitlist and re-queues them to socket receive queue

local_validation.patch_context: Inserted immediately before __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist); at old line 303/new line 315. Uses skb_queue_walk_safe (safe against unlinking during traversal).
local_validation.effect: io_uring SKBs survive GC and remain valid until io_uring context releases them

evidence_items.value: Post-purge loop moves io_uring GC candidates from gc_candidates back to gc_inflight_list to prevent their destruction

local_validation.patch_context: Inserted after __skb_queue_purge and spin_lock(&unix_gc_lock), before the BUG_ON(!list_empty(&gc_candidates)) assertion. Without this loop, io_uring candidates would trigger the BUG_ON or be otherwise mishandled.
local_validation.rationale: io_uring registered files need cycle detection (they participate in GC marking phase) but must survive GC to be cleaned by io_uring's own teardown

evidence_items.value: Fix commit references Fixes: 6b06314c47e1 ("io_uring: add file set registration") as the commit that introduced the vulnerable io_uring file registration mechanism

local_validation.note: The Fixes tag identifies the commit that introduced the io_uring file registration feature, which created the necessary precondition for the UAF when combined with unix_gc's unaware purge behavior

block_reasons: Fixes tag points to the feature introduction commit, not the vulnerability introduction point. The actual vulnerability arises from the interaction between unix_gc and io_uring file registration, not from the feature commit alone.

evidence_items.value: unix_gc treats SKBs in hitlist as universally safe to free, but io_uring maintains a separate reference domain (registered file table) that GC is unaware of, making any SKB carrying io_uring-registered files unsafe to free through the GC path

source_refs.snippet: __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist); // freed SKBs including io_uring ones before fix
skb->scm_io_uring = 1; // marks io_uring ownership of SKB

local_validation.reasoning: The fix introduces a flag to differentiate io_uring SKBs from regular SCM SKBs, proving that the root cause is the lack of this differentiation. The two-part fix (pre-purge extraction + post-purge re-inflight) confirms that both the skb-level and unix_sock-level tracking needed protection.

evidence_items.value: Race window in scr24x_open() between container_of(inode->i_cdev) and kref_get(&dev->refcnt) allows scr24x_remove() to kfree(dev) via kref_put before open can increment the refcount

local_validation.notes: The race window between lines 87 and 88 of scr24x_open: container_of obtains dev, then kref_get tries to increment. If scr24x_remove at line 304 calls kref_put during this window, dev is freed by scr24x_delete (lines 55-59) via kfree(dev). scr24x_open then performs kref_get on freed memory. Additionally, line 90 stores the freed pointer in filp->private_data.

evidence_items.value: The comment in scr24x_release reveals the developers' flawed assumption that no open() can be in-flight after remove's kref_put, which is the root cause of the missing guard

source_refs.snippet:
/* We must not take the dev->lock here as scr24x_delete() might be called
to remove the dev structure altogether.  We don't need the lock anyway,
since after the reference acquired in probe() is released in remove()
the chrdev is already unregistered and noone can possibly acquire
a reference via open() anymore. */

local_validation.notes: The reasoning is incorrect: open() can be mid-execution (between container_of and kref_get) when remove() runs, so 'noone can possibly acquire a reference via open() anymore' is false during the race window.

evidence_items.value: Fix commit deletes the entire driver (vulnerable_branch_removed archetype), not just the racy lines

source_refs.snippet:
char: pcmcia: remove all the drivers

These char PCMCIA drivers are buggy[1] and receive only minimal care. It
was concluded[2], that we should try to remove most pcmcia drivers
completely.

evidence_items.value: Similar race-prone open/remove patterns exist in cm4000_cs.c (cmm_open uses cmm_mutex and pcmcia_dev_present check) and cm4040_cs.c (cm4040_open uses cm4040_mutex and pcmcia_dev_present check), but scr24x_cs.c is the only one lacking any form of synchronization between open and remove

local_validation.notes: scr24x_cs.c's open lacks both the mutex and pcmcia_dev_present check that the other two drivers use. The other drivers also have different patterns of race window protection.

The OCB grandloop processes 6x16-byte blocks per iteration; $len is initialized to (total_length - 96) as an end pointer; $inp advances by 96 each iteration

source_refs.snippet: initializes $len to the true length, less 96 bytes and converts it to a pointer so that the $inp can be compared to it

evidence_items.value: The identical fix (jb to jbe in OCB grandloop) was also applied to the x86_64 variant in a separate commit; this CVE is specifically for the x86 32-bit AES-NI path

evidence_items.value: ossl_policy_cache_set acquires write lock on x->lock, then calls policy_cache_new which calls ossl_policy_cache_set_mapping. When ret == -1 (malformed mapping), bad_mapping: tries to acquire the same write lock again → deadlock on non-recursive Windows lock primitives.

source_refs.snippet: Bug-introducing commit: added CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock to fix Coverity data-race, but created double-lock

evidence_items.value: Fix removes the double-lock block entirely. The redundant EXFLAG_INVALID_POLICY flag setting is already done by caller's bad_cache: label in policy_cache_new (pcy_cache.c:168-169). Pure deletion with no added guards.

source_refs.snippet: Removed 4 lines: if (ret == -1 && CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock(x->lock)) { x->ex_flags |= EXFLAG_INVALID_POLICY; CRYPTO_THREAD_unlock(x->lock); }

evidence_items.value: CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock on Windows uses EnterCriticalSection (non-recursive) or AcquireSRWLockExclusive (non-recursive). Neither supports recursive acquisition; both deadlock on second lock from same thread.

source_refs.snippet: Windows: EnterCriticalSection (CRITICAL_SECTION, non-recursive) or AcquireSRWLockExclusive (SRWLOCK, non-recursive)

evidence_items.value: Commit 9aa4be691f (intended to fix Coverity 1474424 data race) introduced the double-lock by wrapping x->ex_flags assignment in CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock without checking whether the lock is already held by the caller.
xhci_ring_fetch in hw/usb/hcd-xhci.c contains an unbounded while(1) loop that follows LINK TRBs without counting traversals

source_refs.snippet: Vulnerable while(1) loop with unconditional TR_LINK following

evidence_items.value: xhci_ring_chain_length in hw/usb/hcd-xhci.c contains an unbounded while(1) loop that follows LINK TRBs without counting traversals

source_refs.snippet: Vulnerable while(1) loop with unconditional TR_LINK following

evidence_items.value: xhci_ring_fetch TR_LINK else-branch follows link pointer then loops without bound

source_refs.snippet: else { ring->dequeue = xhci_mask64(trb->parameter); if (trb->control & TRB_LK_TC) { ring->ccs = !ring->ccs; } }

evidence_items.value: xhci_ring_chain_length TR_LINK if-branch follows link pointer then continues without bound

source_refs.snippet: if (type == TR_LINK) { dequeue = xhci_mask64(trb.parameter); if (trb.control & TRB_LK_TC) { ccs = !ccs; } continue; }

evidence_items.value: #define TRB_LINK_LIMIT 4 defines the maximum number of consecutive LINK TRBs to process
Guard in xhci_ring_fetch: if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return 0; } breaks circular LINK chains
Guard in xhci_ring_chain_length: if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return -length; } breaks circular LINK chains
Both vulnerable functions iterate through TRB rings; a circular chain of LINK TRBs causes infinite loop in either function because neither counted link traversals before the fix

source_refs.snippet: Needed to avoid we run in circles forever in case the guest builds an endless loop with link trbs.

local_validation.pattern: In each case, an allocated region is orphaned because a specific deallocation call was omitted from the code path.

evidence_items.value: net/queue.c is the root cause file because the reentrancy guard (queue->delivering check) was present in qemu_net_queue_send() but was completely bypassed by the direct nc->info->receive() pattern used in loopback paths across all NIC emulators. The fix extends this guard to qemu_net_queue_receive().

local_validation.patch_observation: The added qemu_net_queue_receive() mirrors qemu_net_queue_send() in checking queue->delivering. The vulnerability exists because the loopback path bypassed qemu_net_queue_send entirely.

evidence_items.value: qemu_can_send_packet (net/net.c) was refactored to delegate to qemu_can_receive_packet(sender->peer). Previously it had inline checks for sender->peer->receive_disabled and sender->peer->can_receive, but these checks examined the peer's state, which is semantically incorrect for the loopback case where sender and receiver are the same NIC.

local_validation.commit_message_confirmation: The commit message explicitly states: 'this is done by calling nc->info->receive() directly which in fact suppresses the effort of reentrancy check that is done in qemu_net_queue_send()'

evidence_items.value: Across 9 NIC drivers, the loopback path calls nc->info->receive() (or a device-specific wrapper) directly instead of routing through qemu_net_queue_send(), bypassing the queue->delivering reentrancy check. Removing removed_critical_sequence lines from 9 files and replacing with qemu_receive_packet() calls.

source_refs.snippet: nc->info->receive(nc, buf, size) -> qemu_receive_packet(nc, buf, size)
nc->info->receive(nc, buf, size) -> qemu_receive_packet(nc, buf, size)
nc->info->receive(nc, buf, size) -> qemu_receive_packet(nc, buf, size)

local_validation.pattern: Every loopback path in every affected file was changed from direct receive call to qemu_receive_packet(). No exception: 9/9 NIC-level patches.

evidence_items.value: qemu_net_queue_receive() and qemu_net_queue_receive_iov() check if queue->delivering is set and return 0 if so, preventing reentrant packet delivery and breaking the infinite recursion.

local_validation.note: This mirrors the same check in qemu_net_queue_send() which was always present for the non-loopback send path, confirming this is the intended reentrancy mechanism.

evidence_items.value: The fix pattern is identical across all 9 NIC drivers and 1 net_tx_pkt helper: every loopback path previously calling nc->info->receive() (or device-specific wrapper) is replaced by qemu_receive_packet() (or qemu_receive_packet_iov()). The core infrastructure commit adds the supporting functions. This is a systematic fix, not an ad-hoc per-device patch.

source_refs.snippet: NIC that supports loopback mode will be converted to this helper.

local_validation.pattern_count: 10 commits, 13 files, 10 replaced vulnerable sequences. All follow same pattern.

block_reasons: The invariant describes the fix pattern but is not itself a certificate for any individual commit's affected-version boundaries. It supports the overall assessment but should not be used as a hard certificate for tag labeling.

evidence_items.value: CVE description states: 'The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.' This matches the CWE-835 infinite loop classification.

local_validation.note: Aligns perfectly with the patch analysis: loopback mode + bypassed reentrancy check = infinite loop.

block_reasons: CVE descriptions can be imprecise. Only use as supplementary context, not as primary evidence.

hunk @@ -1671,6 +1672,13 @@ dissect_gquic_tag — end: label with overflow guard

evidence_items.value: while(tag_number) loop with tag_len=0 stall via attacker-controlled offset_end

source_refs.snippet: while(tag_number) loop with tag_offset=0, total_tag_len=0, no progress invariant

local_validation.check: Verified at parent: tag_offset starts at 0, tag_len = offset_end - tag_offset, default case does tag_offset += tag_len, no check that tag_offset increases. Attack: offset_end == tag_offset → tag_len=0 → tag_offset unchanged → loop continues tag_number times. Fix replaces with goto end; confirming the loop-stall vector.

evidence_items.value: default: case now does goto end; instead of tag_offset += tag_len;

source_refs.snippet: added: goto end; in default: case

local_validation.check: goto end; bypasses the rest of the loop body (including tag_offset accumulation, post-switch check, and tag_number--) and jumps to the end: label which returns immediately. This prevents any further iterations for unknown tags.

evidence_items.value: if (offset + total_tag_len <= offset) overflow guard at end: label

source_refs.snippet: if (offset + total_tag_len <= offset) { expert_add_info_format(pinfo, gquic_tree, &ei_gquic_length_invalid, "Invalid total tag length: %u", total_tag_len); return offset + tvb_reported_length_remaining(tvb, offset); }

local_validation.check: The condition offset + total_tag_len <= offset catches both total_tag_len=0 (zero-length) and wraparound where offset + total_tag_len overflows guint32. Returns remaining packet length as a safe skip instead of the corrupted offset.

evidence_items.value: while(tag_number) loop lacks tag_offset advancement invariant; attacker controls offset_end per iteration per packet data

source_refs.snippet: Full vulnerable function: while(tag_number) reads offset_end from packet per iteration, tag_len=offset_end-tag_offset, default: advances tag_offset by tag_len

local_validation.check: Each loop iteration reads a new offset_end from packet at the advancing 'offset' pointer (always +8 bytes per iteration), giving the attacker fresh control over offset_end values. tag_offset only advances by tag_len. When offset_end==tag_offset, tag_len=0, tag_offset unchanged. No invariant check prevents this.

evidence_items.value: Commit message: 'GQUIC: make sure our tag offset advances. Make sure gquic tag offset advances so that we don't infinitely loop.'

source_refs.snippet: GQUIC: make sure our tag offset advances. Make sure gquic tag offset advances so that we don't infinitely loop.

local_validation.check: Commit message explicitly states 'infinitely loop' as vulnerability symptom, matching CWE-835. Root cause 'tag offset not advancing' confirmed via code analysis.

block_reasons: Commit message describes symptom (infinite loop) rather than fully characterizing the mechanism; root cause validated via code evidence
